smartlead_campaign: OR #4 | Ecom Leads | Scroll Stopper | NO Personlization

magnet.report_label: Scroll-Stopper Sheet (the 3 ads we wrote them)

thread.subject: 3 ads for Barrelhand
thread.body_text:
hey Karel, spent a couple minutes on the Barrelhand site and honestly can't figure out why we're not already working together haha.

anyway, that's not why I'm writing. I run Meta ads for ecom brands, and instead of pitching you some BS, I actually built you a few. (gotta provide value before anything)

built 3 ad mockups from Barrelhand's real products, plus a world-class brand playbook I pulled from your site and reviews. it's done and sitting ready on my end.

want me to send it over?

- Kyle

Hi Kyle, 

 We are currently running meta ads, would be curious what you had in mind and how you would approach it differently.
 

 -Karel

here you go Karel - 3 ad mockups built from Barrelhand's own products, plus your brand playbook. 

 the mockups show the angles, the playbook is the part most brands actually keep - your voice, the words your customers use, and what you can and can't say in ad copy.

 if any of them click, the quickest way to see how I'd actually run them is a 30 min call. → Calendar 

 - Kyle

copy.tldr: Barrelhand sells a $9,750 spaceflight-grade mechanical watch with no visible reviews and no ads we can find, so this call is about building demand from zero for a high-ticket collector product, not fixing a broken account.

deep_dive.what_they_are: An engineering-first independent watch brand out of San Francisco. They frame themselves as a platform for space exploration tools, not a heritage luxury house. The site is more manifesto than store right now.
deep_dive.what_they_sell: One main product we can name: the Monolith, a mechanical toolwatch priced at $9,750, offered in three strap colorways (Barrelhand Orange, Black, and Black & Orange). They also mention a Memory Disc initiative and a 'Project One'. Next batch deliveries start Q4 2026 and orders are fully refundable before shipment.
deep_dive.positioning: Top-tier, technical, and mission-driven. Built to endure, serviceable over time, made for the frontier. The tone is poetic but grounded in engineering, not old-world luxury.
deep_dive.who_buys: Serious watch collectors and space or technology enthusiasts with high disposable income. People who like being early to a frontier story and who value provable engineering over brand heritage.
strengths: A single clear hero product at a premium price, with a strong story and a distinct look. That makes ad angles easy to build around.
A refundable pre-order model and a Q4 2026 delivery window, which gives a natural reason to buy now without faking urgency.
gaps: No reviews or social proof anywhere on the site. For a $9,750 purchase, buyers need trust signals before they will ever convert from an ad.
No Meta ads we could find running. They told Kyle they are running Meta ads, so either they are new, paused, or run in a way we cannot see. This has to be confirmed on the call.

game_plan.headline: Start from zero: use the Scroll-Stopper ads to find one working angle for the Monolith, then build trust and widen the audience before we spend real money.

phases.goal: Prove that paid traffic can produce interest and email signups for the Monolith at a sane cost. At this price point we are not chasing day-one sales, we are proving the top of the funnel works.
campaigns: One cold prospecting campaign built on the 3 Scroll-Stopper ads, testing them against each other.
A simple retargeting campaign for anyone who visits the site or watches the video but does not buy.
lead_products: The Monolith is the only product to lead with. It is the one product with a public price, three named colorways, and a full story on the site. It is effectively the bestseller by default because it is the offer they actually sell today.
phases.creative: Run the 3 ads from the Scroll-Stopper Sheet first. Lead with the ad that carries the strongest single idea (the 'spaceship for your wrist' engineering angle) because at $9,750 the story has to do the selling, not a discount. Send everyone to a page that captures email for early access, since most people will not buy a five-figure watch on first click.
phases.budget: Start small and hold it steady for the full 30 days so we can read results.
phases.what_success_looks_like: One of the three ads clearly beats the others on cost per click and email signup, and we can see people from the ads spending real time on the Monolith page. Actual sales in month one are a bonus, not the target.

phases.goal: Put more budget behind the winning ad and widen who sees it, while fixing the trust gap that will block sales at this price.
campaigns: Scale the winning ad and add two new variations of its angle.
Retargeting that leans on trust: founder story, engineering detail, serviceability, refundable pre-order.
A warm campaign to the email list built in month one.
lead_products: Still the Monolith. We do not add a second product until the first one is proven to sell.
phases.creative: Build new ads around whatever angle won in month one. Add proof: close-up engineering shots, the refundable pre-order, the Q4 2026 delivery. If they have any collector or press mentions, we put those to work here since the site has no reviews.
phases.budget: Raise budget only on the proven winner. Keep testing money separate and small.
phases.what_success_looks_like: First sales or serious buyer inquiries traced back to ads, a growing email list, and a cost per lead that is steady or falling as we spend more.

phases.goal: Scale the proven angle and introduce a second offer to the audience we have built.
campaigns: Scale the winning campaign to a higher steady budget.
Introduce a second product or entry point to the warm audience.
Full retargeting funnel across cold, warm, and site visitors.
lead_products: Monolith stays the hero. The second offer would be the Memory Disc initiative or a lower-commitment entry point like the newsletter early-access list or Monolith Source Files, so we can bring in people who love the brand but are not ready for a $9,750 buy.
phases.creative: Best-performing Monolith ads plus fresh creative for the second offer. Use the email list and past site visitors to sell the second product cheaply.
phases.budget: Scale steadily on winners. Never jump spend fast on a five-figure product; the sales cycle is long.
phases.what_success_looks_like: A repeatable cost to acquire a Monolith buyer or lead, a second product bringing in cheaper leads, and a plan to keep scaling into 2027 as Q4 2026 deliveries begin.

estimates.label: Starting budget
estimates.value: About $100 to $150 a day to start
estimates.basis: Enough to get a few dozen clicks a day at typical costs, which is the floor for learning which of the three ads works. This is a learning budget, not a scale budget.

estimates.label: Revenue we would aim to add
estimates.value: We cannot give an honest number yet
estimates.basis: At a $9,750 price and no visible past ad results, revenue depends on their conversion rate and how long a buyer takes to decide, neither of which we can see. We would set a target after we confirm their current numbers on the call.

assumptions: The Monolith is their main product and the one they most want to sell right now.
They can accept pre-orders and refund before shipment as the site states, so a sale does not require finished stock in hand.
There is enough of a global collector and space-enthusiast audience on Meta to build a cold audience at this price.
The long decision cycle on a $9,750 watch means email capture and retargeting matter more than first-click sales.

questions.question: You mentioned you're running Meta ads now. Can you walk me through what that looks like today, what you spend, and who's building the ads?
questions.listening_for: Whether ads are actually live, roughly what they spend, and if it's them, a freelancer, or an agency. We found no ads, so their answer tells us if they're new, paused, or running in a way we couldn't see.

questions.question: When you've run ads before, what happened? Did anything sell from them?
questions.listening_for: Any past result, good or bad. If they've never sold a watch from ads, we're starting from zero and month one is about learning, not revenue.

questions.question: On a $9,750 watch, what does your margin look like after the build cost? How much room is there to spend to get one buyer?
questions.listening_for: Whether the margin can carry paid ads. A high-ticket product usually can, but we need to hear it from them, not assume.

questions.question: How many Monoliths do you need to sell to feel this is working, and by when?
questions.listening_for: Their real expectation. If they want fast volume on a five-figure watch, we need to reset that on this call.

questions.question: When someone lands on your site interested in the Monolith, what happens next? Do they buy, book a call, or join the list?
questions.listening_for: Whether there's a path to convert. If the only action is a $9,750 checkout with no lower step, that's the leak, and it changes month one to focus on email capture.

questions.question: You have no reviews or buyer stories on the site. Do you have any owners, collectors, or press we could point to?
questions.listening_for: Any proof at all we can put in retargeting ads. No proof on a five-figure watch is the single biggest blocker to sales.

questions.question: How long does a typical buyer take to decide? Days, weeks, months?
questions.listening_for: The length of the sales cycle. A long cycle means we judge month one on leads and site engagement, not sales, and we lean hard on retargeting.

questions.question: Of the three ads we sent, did any one of them feel most like Barrelhand to you?
questions.listening_for: Their gut on the angle. It tells us which ad to lead with and confirms whether our read of their voice is right.

questions.question: Do you get people finding you on Google by searching things like 'space watch' or 'spaceflight watch', or is it mostly people who already know the brand?
questions.listening_for: Whether there's real search demand. Space and toolwatch terms do get searched, so if buyers are already looking, Google search ads could catch people lower in the funnel who are ready to buy. But if it's all direct brand traffic, we hold off.

questions.question: Are you looking to be found for terms like 'independent watchmaker' or 'aerospace watch' over the next year, or is that not a priority yet?
questions.listening_for: Interest in SEO. Their story-heavy site could rank over time, but this is a slow channel and only worth raising if they think long term. Don't force it if they want fast results.

objections.objection: We already run Meta ads, why would we switch to you?
objections.answer: That's fair, and I'm not assuming yours are broken. I couldn't find your ads running when I looked, so first I'd want to see what's live today. What I brought is three ads built from your own product and voice, ready to test against what you have. If mine don't beat yours, you've lost nothing.

objections.objection: Nobody buys a $9,750 watch off a Facebook ad.
objections.answer: You're right, and that's exactly why month one isn't about the sale. At this price the ad's job is to find the right person and get them onto your list. Then we build trust over weeks with follow-up ads. The sale happens later, on their timeline, not on first click.

objections.objection: We don't have reviews or big-name proof yet, doesn't that make this harder?
objections.answer: It does, and I'd rather be straight with you about it. That's why part of the first 60 days is putting your engineering story, the refundable pre-order, and any collector or press mention to work as proof. We build trust into the ads instead of pretending you have hundreds of reviews.

objections.objection: How can you promise revenue when you've never seen our numbers?
objections.answer: I can't, and I won't make one up. I don't have your conversion rate or how long a buyer takes to decide. Once you share those on this call, I'll give you a target I can stand behind. Anyone throwing you a revenue figure before seeing your numbers is guessing.

red_flags.flag: No reviews or social proof anywhere on the site.
red_flags.why_it_matters: A $9,750 purchase needs heavy trust. With nothing to point to, cold traffic will bounce and sales will be slow until we build proof into the funnel. This directly shapes the plan.

red_flags.flag: No Meta ads found running, but they say they run them.
red_flags.why_it_matters: We're guessing about their current setup. Kyle must confirm what's actually live before promising anything, or he'll contradict what they already know.

red_flags.flag: Very high price with a long, considered sales cycle.
red_flags.why_it_matters: Expect few sales and a high cost per buyer early on. If the owner expects fast volume, expectations must be reset on the call or the relationship sours by month two.

red_flags.flag: Deliveries don't start until Q4 2026 and the store may not be a standard checkout.
red_flags.why_it_matters: If buyers can't complete a clean purchase or worry about a far-off delivery, that blocks conversions no ad can fix. We need to confirm the buying path works.

red_flags.flag: Strict list of claims we cannot make (no NASA, no 'flown in space', no fake urgency).
red_flags.why_it_matters: Get this wrong in ad copy and it's a legal and brand problem. Kyle should mention we already know these limits from the playbook to build confidence.

other_channel_openings: Google search ads, but only if buyers are already searching terms like 'space watch' or 'spaceflight watch'. Confirm real search demand on the call before raising it, since much of their traffic may be direct brand searches.
SEO as a slow, long-term play given their story-rich site, worth mentioning only if the owner thinks in years, not months.
